title: Gas turbine MRO and hot-section replacement parts
what_it_is: The maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) market and aftermarket parts supply chain for the installed base of industrial gas turbines — specifically hot-section components (blades, vanes, combustors, transition pieces) that must be replaced at regular intervals (typically every 20,000–40,000 fired hours). Distinct from new-equipment supply: MRO demand is driven by the existing installed base and creates parallel pressure on the same casting and manufacturing capacity as new turbine orders.
hidden_constraint: The MRO market competes directly with new turbine orders for the same single-crystal blade casting capacity (Howmet, Precision Castparts, Chromalloy). As new-turbine orders surge for AI data-centre load, MRO parts lead times also extend — potentially leaving existing gas turbines unable to complete their scheduled overhaul on time, reducing available firm capacity precisely when it is most needed.
exact_process_role: Hot-section replacement parts (blades, vanes, combustor liners) are required to maintain gas turbine availability. Without timely MRO parts, a turbine must be derated or taken offline at its inspection interval — reducing the grid's firm capacity even before new turbines are delivered.
process_or_equipment_dependency: Same manufacturing process as new blades: directional solidification / single-crystal casting at qualified foundries. OEM proprietary parts vs. third-party MRO parts (from Chromalloy, independent service providers). Third-party parts require OEM service agreement waivers or independent qualification.
why_it_matters: As AI load growth drives new turbine orders and extends OEM factory lead times, the same factory pressure extends MRO parts lead times. This creates a double constraint: new turbines are delayed AND existing turbines face MRO parts shortages. The grid's available firm capacity is squeezed from both ends simultaneously.
why_it_is_underappreciated: New-equipment lead times are discussed; MRO parts lead times affecting the existing installed base are almost never mentioned in the same analysis. The interactions between new-build and MRO demand on the same casting capacity create a feedback loop that worsens both.
manufacturing_or_qualification_difficult: OEM-branded parts require OEM factory casting and qualification. Independent MRO parts (Chromalloy, etc.) require their own alloy qualification and OEM service agreement review. Both paths are capacity-constrained.
lead_time_risk: Hot-section parts lead times at GE and Siemens have extended to 18–24 months in current environment. Long-term service agreements (LTSAs) provide priority but do not resolve the underlying capacity constraint.
supply_concentration: OEM hot-section parts: GE Vernova, Siemens Energy, Mitsubishi Power (internal factories via Howmet and Precision Castparts). Third-party MRO: Chromalloy Gas Turbine (private), StandardAero (private), Sulzer (SUN.SW).
chokepoint_or_sanctions_exposure: Moderate. Primarily a capacity-concentration risk exacerbated by the new-turbine demand surge.
purity_or_spec_constraints: Same alloy and inspection standards as new parts. OEM warranty implications for non-OEM parts. Third-party parts must meet OEM dimensional and metallurgical specs or require service agreement amendment.
substitutability: Third-party MRO parts from qualified independent suppliers (Chromalloy) can partially substitute but are subject to OEM service agreement approval and independent qualification requirements.
upstream_dependencies: single-crystal nickel superalloy castings (same as new turbines)
public_equity_relevance: GE Vernova (GEV — aftermarket services segment is high-margin), Siemens Energy (ENR.DE — service revenue critical to profitability), Mitsubishi Power (7011.T), Sulzer (SUN.SW), StandardAero. Howmet Aerospace (HWM) — benefits from MRO demand as a blade casting supplier.
policy_export_control_relevance: LTSA-related parts export controls in defence-adjacent applications. No broad commercial export control on civil gas turbine MRO parts.
failure_mode: New-turbine order surge consumes OEM casting capacity → MRO parts lead times extend → utilities cannot complete scheduled hot-section overhauls on time → turbines derated or offline at inspection interval → available firm capacity reduced while new turbines are still years from delivery.
triggers_to_watch: GE Vernova / Siemens Energy services backlog and parts lead-time commentary
Utility maintenance schedules and forced-outage rate trends

easing_invalidation_conditions: OEM casting capacity expands faster than demand
